summary: McKenzie describes how he built a cheap, effective user survey system for Bingo Card Creator using Wufoo forms, A/B testing, and clever incentivization. Despite running his business for 3.5 years, he realizes he still doesn't have solid data on who his actual users are beyond his initial customer hypothesis of elementary schoolteachers. He details the specific questions asked, the implementation using Rails and his A/B testing framework, and how he incentivized survey completion by offering users extra free cards at zero marginal cost. He also argues controversially that users don't actually care about privacy despite claiming otherwise, and that businesses should prioritize what users demonstrably want over stated privacy preferences.
key_points: After 3.5 years running a business, McKenzie still relied more on guesses than data about who his actual customers were, discovering belatedly that baby shower bingo was hugely popular
Offering users something that costs you nothing but has perceived value to them (extra free cards) is a powerful incentive for surveys, links, and other marketing activities

The entire intelligent, incentivized survey system with two A/B tests took only ~20 lines of code and ~20 lines of HTML, compared to enterprise environments where comparable work requires planning meetings and dedicated engineers
Users consistently choose convenience over privacy despite claiming otherwise, and businesses that prioritize privacy over what users demonstrably want create problems for themselves
Survey design should minimize length, integrate naturally into the user experience, and let users dismiss it easily rather than using pop-ups or buried links
money_quotes: Programmers live in a world where data is available in infinite abundance, and hence we often assume that data is valueless.
Yes, I am talking to you, Mr. Three Dollars Is Too Much For A Game On My Six Hundred Dollar Phone.
I tend to default to being generous to my users unless I have a darn good reason otherwise.
There's a place for that in the world, don't get me wrong, but I'm already collecting data.
Given the choice between privacy and convenience, they'll choose convenience every single time, and if you prioritize their privacy over their convenience it is your problem.

My theory for why Google didn't anticipate this failure mode is that Google assumes you care about your customers about as much as Google cares about its customers, and at Google forwarding customer complaints to Siberia would double the likelihood they were actually read.
That was my customer hypothesis, to use a buzzword much remarked on lately, and 3.5 years later I'm still not really sure if it is accurate.
key_insight: You can build surprisingly effective user research systems by combining free tools, smart incentives that cost you nothing but have real perceived value to users, and lightweight integration into existing workflows.
